message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 10
Recent changes: Added rune: 25 to BASE_PRICE

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll perform the Refactor phase analysis for the claim-office implementation.

content.content:
architecture-notes.md
eslint.config.mjs
example-mapping
metrics.json
node_modules
package.json

src

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- No refactoring (justified): hardcoded `{ results: [{ premium: 115 }] }` is canonical "fake it" baby step; mass=2 is minimal; no duplication yet to extract; `runScenario` name matches contract.
5	
6	## After cycle 2
7	- Change: extracted module constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER = 1.1`, `ADMIN_FEE = 5`, and `BASE_PRICE: Record<string, number> = { sword: 100, amulet: 60 }`; replaced ternary `itemType === "amulet" ? 71 : 115` with formula `Math.round(BASE_PRICE[itemType] * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`; destructured `steps` from cast scenario.
8	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent) > APP. Opaque computed literals 71/115 hid the spec's pricing model (base × first-insurance loading + admin fee); now the domain is visible. `Math.round` neutralises IEEE-754 drift (100*1.1 = 110.000…01) for the integer-premium contract; spec uses explicit `ceil(...)` only on discounted prices.
9	- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` lookup table (extend with new item types: staff, potion, rune, moonstone). `FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER` (the 1.10 loading for customers with no prior contract). `ADMIN_FEE` (the flat +5 added after all multipliers).
10	
11	## After cycle 3
12	- Change: added `staff: 80` to `BASE_PRICE`; renamed `itemType` binding to `item` (now `const item = steps[0].items[0]`, then `BASE_PRICE[item.type]`).
13	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Upcoming tests attach modifiers (`cursed`, `enchantment`) to the same item object; naming the whole item rather than just its `type` field prepares the binding to carry those properties without rework. Mass effectively unchanged (~20). Resisted extracting `priceForItem`/`quotePremium` helpers — speculative without a duplication driver (Rule 4).
14	- Naming established: `item` as the unit modifiers attach to (will gain `.cursed`, `.enchantment` shortly).
15	
16	## After cycle 4
17	- No refactoring (justified): `potion: 40` test absorbed entirely by the cycle-2 `BASE_PRICE` lookup table — zero edits to `runScenario` body. No new conditional, no duplication, no naming drift; mass unchanged (~37). Resisted extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `quotePremium(...)` again — still speculative; the cursed/enchantment/loyal-customer/multi-item tests next in line will provide the real driver for where pricing logic should live, and premature extraction would lock the shape before those forces appear.
18	
19	## After cycle 5
20	- Change: renamed local binding `cursedMult` → `cursedMultiplier`; structure of `runScenario` body unchanged (pipeline: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee).
21	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). Module constants are spelled out in full (`FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER`, `CURSED_MULTIPLIER`, `ADMIN_FEE`); the local mirrored their role so it should mirror their spelling. Next cycle adds `enchantmentMultiplier` — having two siblings with consistent naming will make the "apply item modifiers" stage read uniformly and signal the extraction point cleanly when a third modifier arrives. Resisted extracting `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` now: only one modifier exists, so there is no duplication yet (Rule 3 needs a driver; Rule 4 says don't add an abstraction until forced). Mass change negligible (~34, binding name length is free).
22	- Naming established: locals that mirror module-level `*_MULTIPLIER` constants use the full word `Multiplier`, not `Mult`.
23	
24	## After cycle 6
25	- Change: added `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER = 1.3`; extracted helper `itemModifierMultiplier(item: Item): number` that combines `cursed` and high-enchantment factors (each `<cond> ? MULTIPLIER : 1`, multiplied together); renamed `itemBase` → `itemPrice`; `runScenario` body collapses to base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee, with the modifier stage now a single named call.
26	- Rationale: Rule 3 (No Duplication) finally has a driver — the second sibling modifier landed in cycle 6, matching the cycle-5 prediction, so the speculative extraction resisted then is now justified. Rule 2 (Reveal Intent): naming the "apply all item-attribute multipliers" stage makes the pricing pipeline scannable; `itemPrice` is more accurate than `itemBase` once modifiers have been applied. APP rose slightly (~36 → ~38) from the function-call frame, but Rule 2 > APP per project conventions. Cycle 7 (cursed AND enchantment 5) will exercise both branches together with zero edits to `runScenario` body — both factors will simply multiply inside the helper. Cycle-7's `ceil(...)` in the spec formula (220 vs would-be-rounded 220) will need attention: current `Math.round` may give same answer for that specific test by coincidence (100×1.5×1.3×1.1 = 214.5 → round→215, ceil→215; +5 = 220 either way... actually round(214.5)=214 banker's rounding in some langs, but JS Math.round = 215; ceil = 215; both yield 220), so cycle 7 may pass with no change; cycle 9 (`ceil(60 × 0.80 × 1.10) + 5 = 58`; product = 52.8, both round and ceil → 53, +5 = 58 — also coincidence-passes). The real ceil-vs-round driver only appears when loyalty discount lands on a non-`.5` fractional that diverges; defer the round→ceil switch until a test actually fails.
27	- Naming established: `itemModifierMultiplier(item)` is THE place to add new item-attribute multipliers (dragon-material reimbursement in claims will live elsewhere — claim-side, not premium-side; this helper is premium-only). `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_MULTIPLIER` (the >=5 threshold version; if a "low-enchantment" tier ever appears, rename accordingly).
28	
29	## After cycle 7
30	- Change: extracted `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value: number): number` that wraps `Math.ceil(Number(value.toFixed(10)))`; `runScenario` premium line collapses to `ceilInMhpcoFavor(itemPrice * FIRST_INSURANCE_MULTIPLIER) + ADMIN_FEE`.
31	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The spec phrase "round in MHPCO's favour" now appears in the code; the `.toFixed(10)` drift guard (which prevents IEEE-754 noise like `100 * 1.10 = 110.00000000000001` from bumping mathematically-integer prices up a euro) is no longer a magic incantation inlined mid-formula. Module mass roughly unchanged: the `runScenario` body lost ~3 (one constant + two invocations) which moved into the helper. Pre-positions for cycles 8–9 (loyalty discount: `100 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 88`, integer; `60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8`, non-integer needing real ceil) and cycle 10 (rune: `25 × 1.10 = 27.5`, ceil → 28) — every one of those is a `ceilInMhpcoFavor(...)` call now, no further inlining.
32	- Naming established: `ceilInMhpcoFavor(value)` is THE round-up gate for any euro-denominated price after multiplicative steps; use it for the final premium computation and any nested ceiling steps the spec calls out. ADMIN_FEE is added AFTER the ceiling, not inside it (spec: ceil applies to the priced value, then flat +5).
33	
34	## After cycle 8
35	- No refactoring (justified): `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER = 0.8` follows the established `*_MULTIPLIER` module-constant convention; local `loyaltyMultiplier` mirrors it per cycle-5 naming rule; the `customer.yearsWithMHPCO >= 2 ? LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER : 1` ternary sits at `runScenario` level (NOT inside `itemModifierMultiplier`, whose scope stays item-attribute-only per cycle-6). Resisted extracting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: only one customer-side multiplier exists today, so Rule 3 has no driver and Rule 4 says don't add a frame without one. The real extraction driver is the upcoming "two-quote scenario … second amulet quote = 60 × 0.85 + 5" test (cycle-2 forecast), which introduces both a second customer-side modifier AND a quote-index dependency — extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature and need rework. Cycle 9 (loyal amulet, 60 × 0.80 × 1.10 = 52.8 → ceil 53 + 5 = 58) is finally the test where `ceilInMhpcoFavor` (cycle 7) does real work — round vs ceil diverge on a non-`.5` fractional; existing helper handles it with zero edits. Mass essentially unchanged (~41).
36	
37	## After cycle 9
38	- Change: extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`; replaced magic `>= 2` in the loyalty ternary with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`.
39	- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline — every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. The spec's domain phrase "loyal customer ≥2 years" now reads in the code. Mass impact negligible (+1 binding, -1 constant from inline literal moving to named binding). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (cycle-8 reasoning unchanged — the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature, so extracting now locks in the wrong shape). Cycle 10 (rune 33) is a pure `BASE_PRICE` table addition — zero edits to `runScenario` predicted.
40	- Naming established: numeric thresholds that gate a multiplier follow the `*_THRESHOLD` suffix (sibling to `*_MULTIPLIER`); both stand together to read as "if customer crosses THRESHOLD, apply MULTIPLIER".
41

content.text:
Now I'll perform the Refactor phase analysis.

## Refactor Phase — Cycle 10 (rune base price)

### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

- Current function: `runScenario` — handles a scenario with quote steps. Name still fits (will extend to claims later).
- `BASE_PRICE` — well-named lookup table.
- `itemModifierMultiplier`, `ceilInMhpcoFavor`, `LOYALTY_MULTIPLIER`, `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD` — all clear, follow established conventions.
- Recent change: added `rune: 25` to `BASE_PRICE`. The `BASE_PRICE` name accurately covers this new entry (rune is a base item type like sword, amulet, etc.). No rename needed.

**Decision:** Keep all current names — they continue to clearly reveal intent.

### Step 2: Calculate Current APP Mass (runScenario body + new BASE_PRICE entry)

The cycle-10 delta is a single key-value pair added to `BASE_PRICE`:
```
rune: 25
```
- Bindings: 1 (`rune` key) = 1
- Constants: 1 (literal `25`) = 1
- Subtotal added: 2

`runScenario` body unchanged from cycle 9. Module-level mass essentially unchanged (~42, +2 for new constant/binding).

### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1 (Tests Pass):**

content.text:
10/10 passing.

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent):**
- Pricing pipeline reads top-to-bottom: base lookup → item-modifier multiplier → loyalty multiplier → first-insurance loading + admin fee.
- Every numeric is a named constant (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 2, 5).
- `ceilInMhpcoFavor` names the round-up semantics and IEEE-754 guard.
- No clarity gap I can spot for current 10 tests.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication):**
- No duplicated logic — only one site per concept.
- Resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (per cycles 8–9 reasoning): the upcoming two-quote test (cycle 14 forecast) will introduce a quote-index-dependent customer multiplier ("post-first-contract discount of 0.85"). Extracting now with only `customer` as parameter would lock in the wrong signature.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements):**
- Cycle 10 added only a single key-value pair to an existing table. No new function frames, no new conditionals. Minimal possible.

### Step 4: Attempt Refactoring

Looking for *any* improvement in the current state:

**Candidate 1:** Extract `priceForItem(item)` that wraps `BASE_PRICE[item.type] * itemModifierMultiplier(item)`.
- Pros: collapses two operations into one named concept.
- Cons: speculative — building-block tests (cycles 11–13) will fundamentally change how items contribute (three alike runes pool to one "building block" priced like an amulet). The signature `priceForItem(item)` will need rework to handle item groups. Extracting now locks the wrong shape. **Reject (Rule 4 + cycle-3/8 precedent on premature extraction).**

**Candidate 2:** Inline `loyaltyMultiplier` local into the formula.
- Hurts Rule 2 (the named local documents what the ternary computes).
- Mass reduction trivial. **Reject (Rule 2 > APP).**

**Candidate 3:** Sort `BASE_PRICE` entries by price (descending or ascending) for visual scannability.
- Current order: sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25 — insertion order, not by value.
- Ascending or descending order would let a reader scan price relationships at a glance.
- Pure presentation change, zero behavioural risk, zero mass change.

Apply Candidate 3 — sort `BASE_PRICE` by value descending.

Tests stay green (10/10). Task list is current — Test 10 is the active one I'm refactoring for; I'll mark it completed after appending architecture notes.

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

Mass unchanged — same keys, same values, same operations; only object-literal key ordering differs. Module mass remains ~42.

### Step 6: Append to Architecture Notes

The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline — every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. input.new_string:
## After cycle 9
- Change: extracted module constant `LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD = 2`; replaced magic `>= 2` in the loyalty ternary with `>= LOYALTY_YEARS_THRESHOLD`.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent). The `2` was the last unnamed numeric in the pricing pipeline — every other parameter (1.10, 1.50, 1.30, 0.80, 5) is a named module constant; the loyalty threshold was the odd one out. The spec's domain phrase "loyal customer ≥2 years" now reads in the code. Mass impact negligible (+1 binding, -1 constant from inline literal moving to named binding). Still resisting `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` extraction (cycle-8 reasoning unchanged — the upcoming two-quote test will force a non-customer-only signature, so extracting now locks in the wrong shape). Cycle 10 (rune 33) is a pure `BASE_PRICE` table addition — zero edits to `runScenario` predicted.
- Naming established: numeric thresholds that gate a multiplier follow the `*_THRESHOLD` suffix (sibling to `*_MULTIPLIER`); both stand together to read as "if customer crosses THRESHOLD, apply MULTIPLIER".

## After cycle 10
- Change: reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries from insertion order (sword 100, amulet 60, staff 80, potion 40, rune 25) to descending-by-value (sword 100, staff 80, amulet 60, potion 40, rune 25). `runScenario` body untouched. Cycle-10 delta itself (`rune: 25`) was absorbed by the cycle-2 lookup table with zero edits to the pricing pipeline, as predicted in cycle 9.
- Rationale: Rule 2 (Reveal Intent), presentation tier. With five entries now, insertion order no longer carries meaning (sword and amulet were just the first two test cases); descending-by-value lets a reader scan the price ladder at a glance and immediately see which items are cheap (rune) vs premium (sword). Zero behavioural risk — `Record<string, number>` key order doesn't affect lookup semantics. Mass unchanged (~42). Resisted (again) extracting `priceForItem(item)` / `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)`: cycles 11–13 (building blocks: three alike runes pool to one amulet-priced unit) will fundamentally change how items contribute to the base price — `priceForItem` with an `Item` signature locks in the wrong shape because building blocks operate on item *groups*, not single items. Cycle 14 (two-quote scenario with post-first-contract discount 0.85) introduces a quote-index dependency that will force the customer-discount extraction with the right signature. Defer both extractions until those tests provide the driver.
- Naming established: `BASE_PRICE` table is sorted descending by value; new entries (moonstone in cycle 13) should be inserted at the correct position to preserve the price ladder.

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring Applied:**
- Reordered `BASE_PRICE` entries descending-by-value (sword 100 → staff 80 → amulet 60 → potion 40 → rune 25). With 5 entries, insertion order had become noise; a sorted ladder is scannable at a glance.

**Naming evaluation:** All names still fit (no rename needed).

**Mass Change:** ~42 → ~42 (key reordering is mass-neutral; the cycle-10 spec delta `rune: 25` itself added +2 to the table).

**Tests:** 10/10 passing.

**Extractions deferred (with reasoning):**
- `priceForItem(item)` — cycles 11–13 (building blocks) will make items contribute in *groups*, not individually; extracting now locks the wrong signature.
- `customerDiscountMultiplier(customer)` — cycle 14 (two-quote scenario, post-first-contract 0.85 discount) introduces quote-index dependency that demands a non-customer-only signature.

Relevant files:
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_19-46-52_claim-office-lite-prose_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/architecture-notes.md`

Proceeding to the next test.
